units.notes: Both rubric criteria are satisfied with directly quotable, file-backed evidence. The critical test-first criterion is met unambiguously: lib/location/url.test.ts line 7 ('written BEFORE lib/location/url.ts exists ... They MUST fail today') and lib/i18n/i18n.test.ts line 3, both with @trace tags binding assertions to spec requirements, and the tests assert spec scenario behavior (deep-link ok/fallback, boundary coords, no-exclamation tone) not the final code shape; tasks 2.4 even distinguishes assertion-RED from import-error-RED. The full lifecycle is evidenced — spec.md present, red tests precede existing impl files (green), review-findings.json shows clean:true with 3 maker-not-checker reviewer passes and review-driven regression tests folded back in, and the change is archived. Minor non-gating deduction: the review note defers the full security sweep to a later global gate (G7) and adversarially rejects some findings, which is reasoned and documented but slightly short of a fully self-contained in-slice review.

Both test files contain only meaningful, non-tautological assertions running against real implementations that postdate the documented RED contract; there is no skip/only/todo, no expect(true), and the review-driven changes added regression tests (trim round-trip, exponential-notation) that tightened rather than weakened coverage, with the implementation changed to satisfy them. Edge, boundary, invalid, and locale cases are exhaustively asserted (±90/±180 accept vs 90.0001 reject, missing/blank/NaN/out-of-range, Ukrainian round-trip, calm-tone across both uk and en tables). The only minor blemish is that several valid-path and just-over-boundary cases assert only the .ok boolean rather than the full discriminated result, slightly less exacting than the flagship toEqual cases — a small deduction, not a miss. Both criteria including the critical one are met.

I verified the slice's actual files against the spec it owns. The changed-files surface (app shell, components/shell, components/ui primitives, lib/i18n, lib/location, lib/utils) maps cleanly onto the app-shell spec's eight requirements, and the only lib domains touched (location, i18n) are exactly those the spec scenarios cite; file headers explicitly anchor each cross-file addition to a spec ID or tech constraint (FR-SHELL-03, TC-STACK-02), so cross-module edits are justified rather than silent drift. The review-driven fixes are all narrowly tied to the slice's own correctness/a11y scenarios and carry regression tests, with the clock left as a documented forward seam rather than an implementation of a later slice — no opportunistic rewrite of unrelated domains. I held back from 100 only because the shared i18n tables and components/ui primitives are a natural place for future scope creep and the in-scope discipline rests partly on the foundation-slice framing, but the evidence shows that boundary was respected here.

Direct inspection of every in-scope source and test file confirms both criteria. Grep for TODO/FIXME/HACK returned zero matches, and the only commented '//' lines are explanatory documentation or deliberate test-first API-contract narration — no commented-out code blocks. The git history shows forward-only review-driven refinement (exponential round-trip, name trim, conditional clock slot, real h1), each backed by a regression test rather than revert churn, and no abandoned half-implementations appear in the diff. Held just below 90+-shippable-perfect only because the verification rests partly on the provided process summary for commit ordering rather than a live git log I could re-run, but the file-level evidence is fully consistent with it.

The test-first evidence is strong and specific: comfort.test.ts carries a '@trace FR-COMFORT-01..03' header explicitly stating it was 'written BEFORE lib/scoring/comfort.ts exists ... MUST fail today,' and its assertions encode spec-derived behavioral contracts (purity/no-throw, determinism via toEqual, no-input-mutation, EMOJI_RE detector, integer 0..100, <=80-char Ukrainian rationale) rather than the final code shape — these map directly to the owned spec scenarios (Total & safe scoring, Ukrainian rationale, Pure framework-free function). The commit sequence cleanly realizes spec(9c9528d scaffold proposal/design/tasks+spec delta) -> tests/red+implement/green(705d996 feat) -> review(09bc3c0 adds missing ComfortBadge render test plus clean review evidence) -> archive(7db3e95), and REVIEW-FINDINGS.json is present with clean=true, two confirmed findings fixed and verified green, baseRef pinned. Minor deductions: the @trace 'written BEFORE' is asserted in a comment rather than independently verifiable via a distinct RED commit (tests and implementation land together in 705d996), and the review-driven ComfortBadge test reveals FR-COMFORT-04 coverage was initially missing — a small gap in the original test-first net that was caught only at review.

Direct inspection of all four test files plus their implementations confirms both rubric criteria are satisfied to a shippable standard. The critical no-weakening criterion is firmly met: zero skip/only/tautological patterns exist, assertions are substantive (non-mutation snapshots, independent boundary cross-checks, 'no dead weight' input wiring), implementations genuinely match the embedded contracts, and the lone review change was an added render test rather than a relaxation, with RED-first confirmed in tasks.md and review-findings.json clean. Edge/locale/boundary coverage is exhaustive — half-open boundaries at 70/69/40/39, full 0..100 sweep, multi-timezone invariance, round-half-up at .5 means, and a broad NaN/Infinity/non-numeric/null safety battery — far beyond the happy path. The minor deduction reflects that two UI-placement spec scenarios (weekend-at-top-of-grid, no-threshold-control) are deferred to the forecast slice and so are not asserted here, though that deferral is in-scope-appropriate and their underlying logic is fully tested.

All in-scope source changes (lib/scoring/{comfort,band,weekend}.ts, components/comfort/ComfortBadge.tsx and their tests) sit inside the comfort-score slice's own domain, and every cross-module touch is justified rather than silent: the i18n edits are additive comfort-only keys (uk.ts:50-60, en.ts:48-56) that the badge directly consumes and that the proposal explicitly listed, and the vitest.config.ts change is the minimal wiring needed to run the slice's own review-added render test. I verified the i18n files contain no rewritten neighbouring keys and the implementation modules carry no opportunistic refactor. The only footprint beyond the comfort domain proper is the global vitest config touch, which is small, justified, and test-scoped — a minor deduction from a perfect score rather than a violation of either criterion.

Direct inspection of every in-scope changed file (comfort.ts, band.ts, weekend.ts, ComfortBadge.tsx, the four test files, i18n en/uk, vitest.config.ts) confirms both craft criteria. Grep found zero TODO/FIXME/dead-code/console artifacts; the comment blocks that grep surfaced are intentional API-contract documentation in test headers, not orphaned code. The history is a clean 4-commit linear progression with no reverts, every export is exercised by a test, and there is no half-built or abandoned code. Held just below 100 because, as an output-only judge, I cannot independently verify the full diff hunks of the i18n/vitest baseline (only the final file states), so I do not award a perfect ship-unchanged score on unseen intermediate churn.

Both criteria are satisfied by direct, quotable evidence. The critical test-first criterion is met: both test files carry explicit RED preambles ('written BEFORE ... exists ... MUST fail today') with `@trace FR-JOKES-01`, and their assertions map one-to-one onto spec.md scenarios (determinism, bounds for 0/large/negative, rotation, empty-list calm fallback, Ukrainian/no-exclamation) rather than to incidental implementation details. The full ordered pipeline is evidenced by commit sequence, tasks.md (red §2 with a confirm-RED step before green §3, 16/16 checked through archive), and a present, clean review-findings.json (clean=true, 0 confirmed across 5 findings). Held just below 95 because RED status rests on the test-header narrative and tasks checkbox rather than a captured failing test run in the evidence.

I read the actual test and implementation files rather than trusting the summary. The critical criterion is met: there are no skipped/disabled/tautological assertions, tests import and exercise the real production modules (including the curated 12-entry JOKES default path), and review evidence shows a clean pass with adversarial findings rejected rather than tests softened. The non-critical criterion is met in depth: sign-safe modulo across negative/huge indices, empty-list calm fallback, Ukrainian-locale enforcement (Cyrillic-only, no Latin/emoji/exclamation), and leap-year day-of-year boundaries are all explicitly asserted with descriptive messages. The tests are test-first RED contracts that genuinely constrain the implementation; I withhold a few points only because true RED-then-green ordering can't be re-verified from a static snapshot, but every visible signal supports it.

Verified against the live repo: the only source touched is the slice's own lib/jokes domain plus a single, tasks.md-scoped and spec-justified joke line in components/shell/Footer.tsx, where pre-existing footer credits and i18n imports are reused unchanged rather than rewritten. The slice notably avoided spilling into the shared lib/i18n module (jokes are kept as local literals in lib/jokes), which is the cleanest possible boundary handling for the in-scope dimension. Both non-critical criteria are clearly met; I hold back a few points only because the process evidence's file list named a standalone lib/jokes/dayOfYear.ts that does not exist (dayOfYear actually lives in select.ts), a minor inaccuracy in the evidence rather than a scope defect.

Both non-critical craft criteria are met against direct file evidence: a grep across all in-scope source returned zero TODO/FIXME/dead-code/commented-out-code markers, and the surviving //-comments are deliberate requirement-traced doc comments and signposted API-contract blocks, not abandoned code. The commit sequence is a clean scaffold->feat->archive arc with no reverts, tasks are 16/16, the Footer fully consumes the selector, and all tests import resolvable modules with no stubs. The only blemish is an evidence-labeling imprecision (process evidence lists a 'lib/jokes/dayOfYear.ts' source file that does not exist on disk — dayOfYear actually lives in select.ts and its test imports from './select'), but this is a summary inaccuracy, not actual thrash in the diff, so it costs only a few points off a shippable result.

Both rubric criteria are met with directly-verified repository evidence. The test file (lib/clock/format.test.ts) is unambiguously spec-first: it carries the '@trace FR-CLOCK-01' marker, an explicit RED-before-implementation header, and a 'must match exactly' API contract block, and every assertion maps to a named spec scenario (deterministic exact string, zone wall-clock vs UTC, calm non-throwing fallback) rather than to the final code shape. The full lifecycle is evidenced by the spec file, the scaffold→feat→review→archive commit chain, tasks.md 14/14 (RED step 2.2 ordered before GREEN §3), and review-findings.json with clean:true / confirmed:[] inside the archive. The only minor imperfection — the post-review aria-label enhancement and the a11y/teardown/layout-shift scenarios resting on manual G6 smoke rather than automated tests — is documented and consistent with the stated strategy, so it warrants a small deduction but does not threaten either criterion.

The critical criterion is solidly met: the only in-scope suite (C:\\Projects\\dev-ai\\project-factory-test-run-claude-code\\lib\\clock\\format.test.ts) is genuinely test-first with strong, non-tautological assertions (exact-string pin, real wall-clock value '1805', non-mutation, fallback length floor) and shows no weakening, skipping, or deletion. Criterion 2 is only partial: edge cases are deep within the pure formatter (invalid locale/timezone, omitted options, non-mutation) but absent for the spec's other explicit boundary scenarios — layout-shift on digit width change, silent timer teardown on unmount, live advance, and the newly added placeholder a11y branch — none of which have any test (no Clock.test.tsx exists). Passes the gate but lands in the flawed-but-shippable band because a large share of the spec's nameable, testable edge cases are verified by review judgement and CSS rather than assertions. | The gating criterion is clearly met: the in-scope test file contains no weakened, skipped, or tautological assertions, and every test pins concrete, content-bound values (exact '12:05', '1805' wall-clock, no-mutation, four distinct fallback branches that verify a readable time rather than merely not throwing) — written RED-first against a genuine Intl-based implementation. The second criterion is only partially met: edge coverage of the pure formatter is excellent, but several boundary/edge scenarios the spec explicitly names — layout-shift digit-width changes, silent timer teardown on unmount, and weather-location scope exclusions — are not asserted in any automated test, with teardown deferred to a manual smoke. With the critical criterion satisfied but one non-critical criterion materially partial, this lands in the high-70s rather than ship-unchanged territory.

All changed files map onto the explicitly declared scope: the slice's own clock domain plus the pre-existing TopBar/layout clock-slot wiring named in proposal.md Impact and tasks.md 3.3, where the slot was designed upstream by add-app-shell ('clock arrives in the top-clock slice'). No shell internals were rewritten and no unrelated refactor was bundled in — both shell-adjacent edits are minimal slot-filling. The only blemish is the post-review aria-label label+value enhancement, which exceeds design.md's stated `aria-label={t('clockRegionLabel')}` without amending the design, but it stays inside the slice's own component and its own accessibility requirement, so it is in-domain polish rather than scope drift — a minor deduction, not a violation.

I verified the actual in-scope files rather than trusting the summary: a Grep for TODO/FIXME/HACK/@ts-ignore/console.log returned no matches, and every file (format.ts, Clock.tsx, TopBar.tsx, layout.tsx) reads as finished, fully-wired code with only explanatory doc-comments, satisfying criterion 1. The git arc is clean and monotonic with no revert churn and the feature is complete (layout.tsx mounts <Clock/>, clockRegionLabel resolves in both locale tables, the post-review a11y polish is coherently present), satisfying criterion 2. Both non-critical criteria are fully met with no partials; I withhold a few points only because the TopBar comment 'the functional clock arrives in the top-clock slice' is mildly stale narrative, a minor craft nit rather than a rubric miss.

Both rubric criteria are satisfied with directly verifiable evidence. The critical test-first criterion is met: both test files explicitly state they were written before the implementation modules existed, carry @trace tags to FR-ANIM-01/02, define an API contract the implementation matches, and their assertions track spec scenarios (condition->particle mapping, location-not-visitor-clock via a host-TZ loop, fail-calm fallbacks) rather than the code shape — and tasks.md orders 'confirm RED' before implementation. The full spec->tests(red)->green->review->archive flow is evidenced by the 4-commit sequence, review-findings.json with clean:true from two fresh reviewers (one confirmed major fixed, one refuted), and 17/17 tasks complete through archive. Minor non-rubric blemish: the archived spec.md still carries a 'TBD' placeholder Purpose, which does not affect either graded criterion; held just below 95 for that residual sloppiness.

The critical criterion is clearly met: scene.test.ts and daynight.test.ts assert specific, meaningful values (per-code particle kinds, distinct day/night gradients, closed four-kind particle set, determinism) with no tautologies, skips, or deletions, and the review fix was an implementation change rather than a test weakening — confirmed against the real, substantive lib/sky implementation. Edge/boundary/locale coverage of the pure helpers is exemplary: inclusive sunrise / exclusive sunset boundaries, a four-zone TZ matrix, an explicit anti-UTC-slice rollover test, and exhaustive NaN/Infinity/non-integer/missing-input fail-calm cases. Points are deducted because two spec edge scenarios (reduced-motion suppression and pointer-events pass-through) are enforced only in CSS/markup with no automated assertion, so the spec's edge cases are not fully covered by tests.

The slice's own domain (lib/sky/* and components/background/AnimatedBackground.tsx) is net-new with no overlap, and every cross-module edit is directly traceable to a spec requirement: page.tsx integration is the only place to wire a location/condition-driven background, globals.css adds CSS that FR-ANIM-03/04 explicitly require be CSS-enforced, and the EmptyState/h1 scrim carries an NFR-A11Y-02 annotation matching the review-confirmed contrast fix in commit aecac62. I confirmed by reading the files that the globals.css and EmptyState changes are strictly additive — pre-existing theme tokens, theme-indicator CSS, and the EmptyState search/i18n contract are left intact — so no opportunistic refactor was smuggled in. Both criteria are met with strong, quoted evidence; the only minor blemish is the scrim leaking into two view files, but it is spec-justified (theme-independent gradient vs. theme-dependent text) rather than drift.

Both non-critical craft criteria are met on direct file evidence, not just the process summary: a content grep across the entire in-scope surface found zero TODO/FIXME/HACK/ts-ignore/eslint-disable markers, and reading every changed source file confirmed only explanatory comments (no commented-out code) and no dead exports — all of index.ts's surface is consumed by page.tsx/AnimatedBackground/scene.ts. The commit history is strictly linear with no revert churn, and the lone review fix (the AA-contrast scrim) is fully and consistently landed in both EmptyState.tsx and page.tsx with no abandoned half-implementation. I withhold the last few points only because 'craft' here is judged on a clean but narrow surface and I cannot independently confirm the tests were actually run green from this static evidence; nothing in the visible artifacts contradicts a ship-ready result.

Both rubric criteria, including the gating critical one, are met and independently verified against the repository rather than taken on faith from the evidence summary. The test files carry explicit test-first (RED) headers and @trace tags, and their assertions are behavioral contracts that map cleanly onto specific spec scenarios (zero-results tolerance, graceful degradation with no literal 'undefined', decorative-only flag) rather than reverse-engineered code shape. The full spec → red → green → two-round review → clean review-findings.json → archive cycle is corroborated by the 6-commit history, a real clean:true findings file with 4 fixedFindings, and source that actually reflects the described ARIA-honesty correction and trim regression fix. Held just below 95 because the one observable softness is that the process leaned on a half-implemented combobox shell in the first implementation pass (caught only in review round 2), and tasks.md's granular checkboxes are described loosely as '17/17' — minor, not defects in the process itself.

The critical integrity criterion is solidly met: I read all three geo test files and the implementation, found no skipped/tautological/deleted tests anywhere in the project, and confirmed the two review rounds added a guarding regression test and removed dishonest ARIA rather than weakening anything. Criterion 2 is only partially met — the unit-testable mapper boundary cases are thoroughly asserted, but a large class of the spec's ~24 edge scenarios (debounce timing, oversized-input bounding, no-key-in-client, deep-link/Enter rules, calm-copy locale, geolocation denial) have no executable assertion at all, existing only as comments in the untested CitySearch component and geocode route. With the critical met but a real coverage gap on non-mapper edge cases, the score lands above the pass line but well short of shippable-unchanged. | The critical anti-cheating criterion is solidly met: the three lib/geo test files are strong, deep-equal, no-throw, non-tautological, and the implementation (trim in map.ts L47-51, non-object skip in parse.ts L31-33) genuinely earns green without any test being bent, skipped, or deleted — the review even hardened honesty by removing fake ARIA from production rather than weakening a test. However, criterion 2 is substantially unmet: this slice's spec owns ~24 scenarios across 10 requirements, but the only tests written cover the pure mappers; the spec's headline behavioral and boundary edges (debounce-race single request, the MAX_QUERY_LENGTH=200 oversized boundary the spec explicitly calls out, whitespace-no-request, Enter-auto-select semantics, zero-results clearing, network-error+retry, and geolocation denial fallback) are all implemented in CitySearch.tsx/route.ts yet asserted nowhere. Because a critical criterion passing keeps the floor above 49 but a major non-critical criterion is largely unmet, the output lands below the pass line.

Verified against the live repo: the slice's source changes (lib/geo/*, components/search/CitySearch.tsx, app/api/geocode/route.ts, scoped lib/i18n keys, minimal app/page.tsx wiring) are all confined to its own domain or justified by the city-search spec, and it consumes lib/location read-only without editing it — satisfying both criteria, neither of which is critical. The review-driven corrections are in-scope fixes to the slice's own files, not opportunistic rewrites of unrelated code. Small deductions: stale documentation left by the in-scope correction — uk.ts line 22 ('The suggestion list is a labelled listbox') and CitySearch.tsx line 13 ('the accessible combobox/listbox') still describe the listbox pattern that was deliberately removed — plus a locally duplicated coordinateLabel helper (CitySearch.tsx line 39) when lib/location/coordinateLabel.ts already exists.

Both rubric criteria are met against the actual source. A targeted grep across all in-scope files (lib/geo, components/search, app/api/geocode) found no TODO/FIXME/dead-code markers, and the only comments are doc prose and test-first signature stubs, not commented-out code. The half-implemented WAI-ARIA combobox/listbox flagged in review is entirely absent from current source (zero live-code grep hits) — the final CitySearch.tsx is a clean native-button list, so the two-round correction shows as honest convergence rather than abandoned thrash in the committed result. Score held just below perfect because the in-source comment density is high, but every comment is load-bearing rationale rather than cruft, so no real defect remains.

Both rubric criteria are met with direct, verifiable evidence. The critical test-first criterion is satisfied: map.test.ts carries @trace IDs, explicitly states it was written before map.ts/types.ts existed and must fail RED then drive implementation, and its assertions track concrete spec scenarios (empty daily, missing hourly temp, null sun times, 11 malformed inputs that must not throw) rather than mirroring final code shape. The full lifecycle is evidenced by the commit sequence, tasks.md gate ordering (confirm RED before green, then review, then archive), a present review-findings.json with clean:true, and a real review-driven correction (CORE-fields-only guard plus a new regression test and an amended invalid-coords spec). The only blemish is a stale non-null type annotation in the test's documentation header that contradicts the corrected nullable contract — a small cleanliness miss, not a process failure.

The critical integrity criterion is firmly met: no skips/tautologies exist, and the review-driven change is demonstrably a strengthening (a new regression test in map.test.ts lines 424-459 plus an unchanged exact-value happy-path test), honoring the file's 'never weaken — strengthen if green' rule. The non-critical breadth criterion is well met at the mapper/route/pipeline layer (malformed inputs, coordinate boundaries, Ukrainian-locale and no-emoji assertions, multi-TZ invariance, and the no-request guard verified via not.toHaveBeenCalled), but is only partially met overall because every forecast React component lacks any test, leaving the retry/accessible-name/console-silent rendering scenarios asserted only as data outcomes. Score 84 reflects an excellent, non-gamed data-layer test process with a real and evidenced gap in user-visible/interaction coverage.

Verified against the repo: lib/weather/map.ts and app/page.tsx match the process evidence exactly, and the only cross-module edits (app/page.tsx integration wiring, i18n strings) are minimal and each tied to a named spec requirement (FR-FORECAST-01..05, FR-SHELL-03) rather than silent drift. The single post-scaffold code change is a review-driven, regression-tested fix to the slice's own mapper, with no sibling-slice source touched and no opportunistic rewrite evident. Both criteria are met; I withhold a perfect score only because the shared i18n module makes precise attribution of newly added vs. pre-existing keys non-trivial from the evidence alone — a small isolation caveat, not a violation.

Direct inspection of every in-scope source file (lib/weather/*.ts and components/forecast/*.{ts,tsx}) plus targeted greps confirms both criteria are met. There are zero TODO/FIXME/HACK markers, no commented-out code (only deliberate prose/contract comments), and no dead code. The one mid-slice correction is a coherent forward fix with a regression test, applied uniformly across the changed type and all its consumers, with the spec contradiction reconciled — no revert churn or half-implementation is visible. I withhold the last few points only because the process narrative is self-reported in places (e.g. 'test-first RED', '250 tests green') that the static file state can corroborate but not fully prove was the actual authoring order.

Both rubric criteria, including the critical one, are met with strong cross-corroborating evidence: the test file header declares test-first/RED intent with an FR-MAP-03 trace, the commit and tasks ordering place failing tests before implementation, and the assertions clearly track spec scenarios (worked example, deep-link round-trip rejection, calm no-exclamation copy) rather than the final code shape. The full spec->red->green->review->archive loop is evidenced by a present review-findings.json with clean:true, fresh dual reviewers, a confirmed minor fix verified green at 272 tests, and the fix itself visible in MapView.tsx and i18n. Score held just below exemplary because I cannot directly observe the RED test-run output or diff timestamps (only the checked task and header assertion), a minor unverifiable gap, but nothing visible contradicts the claimed process.

The pure coordinateLabel test file is genuinely strong — substantive, edge-rich assertions with no skips, tautologies, or weakened cases — and the review-driven implementation fixes are real in the source rather than papered over, so the gating criterion is met. However, the second criterion is substantially unmet: the slice owns ~18 mostly behavioral spec scenarios (tile failure, out-of-range click rejection, popup naming/fallback, keyboard a11y, SSR skeleton/no layout shift, attribution, re-fetch failure, OSM-only tiles) and none of them are asserted — there are no component tests and no test touches any map i18n key, so the review-fixed reject path is itself untested. The '272 tests green' assurance therefore rests on one pure helper plus pre-existing suites, leaving the slice's actual edge/boundary/error behavior unverified, which drops the non-critical dimension below the pass line despite the clean critical result. | The single critical criterion is solidly met: the added tests are dense, concrete value-pinning assertions driving a non-trivial implementation, with no skipped, deleted, tautological, or weakened cases, so the pass gate holds. The non-critical edge-coverage criterion is only partially satisfied — boundary/locale discipline is exemplary for the pure coordinateLabel unit, but the majority of the spec's own reject/error/boundary scenarios (out-of-range click rejection, tile failure, re-fetch failure, SSR skeleton, no-geocoding) have no test and are verified only by manual review. That gap between strong unit-edge coverage and absent spec-scenario edge coverage holds the score just above the pass line rather than into ship-unchanged territory.

Direct source inspection confirms the slice is tightly scoped: coordinateLabel.ts is a new pure module in lib/location reusing (not rewriting) the existing url.ts/types.ts exports, the two components/map files are the slice's own, the i18n change is five additive map-block keys mirrored across en.ts and uk.ts, and the lone app/page.tsx edit is a single import + one MapPanel JSX insertion (the required integration seam). No unrelated refactor, reformatting, or churn in forecast/search/compare/url modules is present. Score held at 90 rather than higher because the cited specs/map/spec.md does not exist in the repo, so the 'justified by spec' clause rests on the self-evident necessity of the seams rather than an independently verifiable spec file.

Both (non-critical) craft criteria are met with directly-quoted evidence. Targeted greps confirm zero TODO/FIXME/HACK/console/ts-ignore in the in-scope map and coordinateLabel sources, and the only comment matches are explanatory header prose, not dead code. The commit arc is clean and forward-only with no reverts, and the single review-driven change (out-of-range inline message + meaningful marker alt) is fully wired across MapView.tsx, en.ts, and uk.ts with no orphaned state. Held just under perfect because the grade rests on the provided diff/commit evidence plus the in-scope files I could read, not the full repo-wide diff.

Verified against the live repo: pins.test.ts and weekendDays.test.ts both carry @trace FR tags, explicit Test-first/RED and contract-first headers, and assertions that track the spec scenarios (limit-at-three, free-a-slot-after-unpin, lat/lon identity dedup, full timezone-invariance sweep) rather than merely the final code shape, satisfying the critical criterion. tasks.md encodes the spec→red→green→review→archive pipeline with all 18 boxes checked, and review-findings.json exists with clean:true, two independent reviewers, and three corroborated minor fixes verified green. The one blemish — strict test-first ordering being unprovable from a same-commit test+impl landing — is honestly disclosed in the trajectory report rather than overclaimed, so it warrants a small deduction, not a gate.

The critical integrity criterion is fully met: no tests are skipped/weakened/tautological, the 'never weaken' discipline is present, and the assertions are specific and meaningful (exact equality, boundary limits, reference identity, immutability snapshots, and a real multi-timezone invariant), with implementations matching the contract. Criterion 2 is only partial — edge/boundary coverage is strong for the two pure logic modules but entirely absent for the UI, a11y, locale, and graceful-degradation scenarios the spec owns, which exist only in untested client components and whose review-flagged fixes rest on manual verification. The gate passes, but the unasserted half of the spec surface (including explicitly review-flagged behaviors) keeps this well below shippable-as-is. | The critical anti-weakening criterion is solidly met: the in-scope unit suites (lib/compare/pins.test.ts, lib/weather/weekendDays.test.ts) contain meaningful, non-tautological assertions backed by real, total implementations, and nothing was skipped or deleted. However, criterion 2 is substantially unmet: edge/locale/a11y/degradation scenarios in the spec live only in .tsx components with no asserting tests, and — most damning for test integrity — the three bugs surfaced in review (dead i18n empty-cell key, stale limit-notice gating, WCAG 2.5.3 label-in-name) were patched in source with no regression test, confirmed by grep finding those identifiers in no test file. With one non-critical criterion largely failed and no UI-layer coverage, the slice scores 62 and does not pass.

Both non-critical in-scope criteria are met against verified repository state. Core logic sits in the slice's own compare and weather(weekendDays) domains, and every cross-module touch (api/forecast/route.ts, lib/weather/index.ts barrel, i18n en/uk, app/page.tsx) is additive and spec-traceable via FR-COMPARE tags, with the trajectory report confirming zero cross-slice module overlap. The single review-driven change is in-scope cleanup of this slice's own dead key, stale notice, and a11y label, not an opportunistic rewrite. I withhold full marks only because the additive/no-overlap conclusion rests partly on the trajectory summary and diff hunks rather than a complete per-file historical diff, and the checker explicitly does not verify test-first ordering.

The slice is genuinely well-crafted: pure, total, documented reducer and weekend selector; coherent composed components; clean linear git history with no revert churn or abandoned half-implementation, satisfying criterion 2. However, criterion 1 is only partially met — I found a dead i18n key 'comparePinAction' defined in both en.ts:83 and uk.ts:95 but referenced nowhere (verified via grep across components/app/tests), which counts as introduced dead code. That this orphan survived a review that explicitly fixed the parallel dead key compareNoWeekendData and still declared clean=true makes the miss more telling, so I dock the non-critical craft score below the ship-unchanged band while keeping it a pass since neither criterion is critical and history quality is strong. | The core source is genuinely clean — no TODO/FIXME/HACK, no console statements, no @ts-ignore/eslint-disable anywhere in scope, and pins.ts/weekendDays.ts/CompareTable/CompareView/PinBar are fully implemented, pure/total, and well-documented (criterion 2 met).
